It's interesting if you look at, you know, like if you listen to Zigg, I mean he's telling pot and pan sale stories that are ancient, right? But that's where the listar is. That's where the audience is, right? And so you want to use your whole storyline, otherwise you get distanced from where they are. One other one? And they didn't come in the room with? Yeah. In my early 20s I worked in the North Sea as a professional diver and one particular season things were particularly lame, a work wise. So effectively I was an unemployed professional diver and I needed a job. So just so happened that I, that's how I got into hydraulics which is the thing now, I guess, you know, make my living problem. So I suppose not that there would be that many unemployed professional divers on my list, but I think there probably, there's a potential there to connect with guys that have a trade background or are mechanics but are looking for specialisation that can take them further in their career. So I'm thinking about, I needed to develop it further. But I think that possibly I could take that as an asset, that sort of unemployed guy with a skillset that wasn't particularly valuable at the time. I got into this thing almost by accident and look where it's taken me. It's taken me a long way further. And I'm looking into what they're doing by accident. Not by design. Especially with this one. Well, yeah. And with no qualifications whatsoever. Okay. I, page 37 and 38 I tossed a couple sample stories in there for you by the way. I didn't call attention to them but I'm going to give you a whole bunch of them at the end of the day but those are out of the unfinished business book. Which by the way, yeah. It's really not hard coming up with 50. You could come up with a lot. Maybe something you're going to talk about maybe I'm just jumping in. How do you start, you know, organizing? Yeah, we'll get there. Yeah, because my actual total list is two pages, two columns. And then you've got to obviously have these things categorised in a way that you can go pull them. So now you're looking for something to illustrate X. Where is it? Right? So principle number two, moving on, is point of view. And this, Charlie goes all the way back. Oh, I started to say incidentally, because it's a little relevant to the story thing. And Charlie said it about his book and the editors wanting to take out the stories. The book I get more mail about than any other book is the unfinished business book. The autobiography book, which of course is all stories. And I get all personal... Any bullet? Somebody else just got it off Amazon. But you've got to use copies. Yeah, Amazon's got to use copies. But people are having trouble getting it from us. So it's on my list of 6,000 things. But... And from that book I get all personal connection stories. So that serves a better purpose. See, that's more useful, more valuable than the... I used your three-letter system and I made $38,000 this month. I mean, those are useful to us as testimonials. But I don't have a connection with that person like I have with the person who writes to me because of the father's story or writes to me because of the alcohol story or writes to me because of the stuttering story or whatever. So there's an interesting story about that. In the first Glenn Turner trial, there were two criminal trials back-to-back. The first one he was represented by F. Lee Bailey, who a lot of people now don't even remember Bailey, but to give you a contemporary reference, he was on the O.J. Simpson Dream Team. And at one time, Bailey was like the criminal attorney guy in the country. And the trial lasted 26 months, I think. And it ended with a hung jury. And a judge kept sending them back when they kept coming out and there was one holdout. And so when the whole thing was over with, you're allowed to talk to the jury and stuff when it's all over with, a criminal trial. So the holdout, what she wanted was for Glenn Turner to come over for dinner and meet her son, who had a hair lip. And so that one human connection kept him from a 10-year jail sentence. And she was like adamant. I mean, everybody else is voting guilty on 422 counts of fraud. And she ain't budging. And it's the power of that human connection that is more powerful than fact, more powerful than resume, more powerful than anything else. Number two, point of view. You have to kind of decide now what you are so that all of your writing is understandable to people from a single position. And so if you think about, here are some of the categories, not all of them, of course. But there are many writers of great influence who are fundamentally observer-reporters. So Napoleon Hill was an observer-reporter. Never claimed to have done anything, never did do anything. The few businesses he attempted all failed. And so his position was telling you about observations and being able to make those observations effectively. Tom Peters in search of excellence is exactly, Tom Peters' position is exactly the same thing. Thomas Stanley, the whole millionaire next door guy, exactly the same thing. People who brought radical system like Atkins. So Atkins' position was as a guy, it's not as radical now as it was in the 60s when Atkins started. But the whole radical, don't count calories, everything you've been told about weight loss is wrong and all you gotta do is not eat carbs. So he came with a unique position. You have a lot of what I would call prophets and psychics, the hairy dents of the world, who are telling you what's going to happen in the future and how you should invest or run your business or whatever. So there's categories to choose from. You can invent a category if you wish, but you do need to get clear on what your primary category is. Doesn't mean you don't fuzzy the edges and doesn't mean you don't become more than one thing to your audience over time. But you have to decide what your primary category is so that you can kind of maintain some consistency and it's easy for them to sort of, okay, I know what this guy is to be. I've got two questions. One is what would you consider yourself in this lesson? The second thing is what would you ask yourself and determine, okay, I need to take this point of view so my reader understands me. Kind of check with you through mentally or otherwise to discover this. One of the things is where are you now? How do they perceive you now? Which is like the exercise he went through at the table is somewhat useful.
